turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: General Mccree, may I ask you this ques- tion , which perhaps I ought to know the answer to but don ' t . What is the purpose of a passport? It's been my own exper- ience in the couple of times I've been abroad other than in country .

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: A passport is currently required both to enter and to leave the United States under an Act of Con- gress in 1978, unless the President provides otherwise by rule . And he has provided otherwise by rule with reference to any country, I believe, in this hemisphere except Cuba , and certain Western European countries do not require it either . But there is such a statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And with reference , I think, to all members of the Armed Services who leave the country under orders , because they ' re ordered to. MR . McCREE: And that would of course --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A soldier doesn't need a passport. MR . McCREE: Of course he does not .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To be sent to West Germany. MR . McCREE: And that's, of course , not inconsis- tent, either, with the presidential rule which exempts non - military personnel from leaving the country.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that if the President should appoint Mr. X as his ambassador plenipotentiary to the Middle East and direct the Secretary of State who is his employee to issue a passport to him, that Congress could pro- hibit the issuance of that passport? MR . McCREE: I think not , and I'm confident he would exclude a person . Certainly, the authorization vested in the President in the Travel Control Act of 1978 would more than cover that, because the President can exempt from the operation of this statute, which forbids departure and reen- try without a passport, according to rules and regulations that he might promulgate . There are -- to reply to Mr . Justice Rehnquist's first question , the passport as I understand it serves two purposes . First, the purpose of identifying the bearer as a cicizen or a national of the issuing nation; and second, to request free passage for him from a foreign nation as well as the efforts of the foreign nation to facilitate his travel .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In its origins, Mr . Solicicor General, was it not in effect a letter of introduction from the President of the United states? MR . McCREE: It was essentially that, Mr . Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And are there not extant passports given 190 and 180 years ago that were personally signed by the President? before 1856 the Congress did not enter this area of passport control at all. Before 1856, che first passport act, the President of the United States, the Secretary of State, and some instances notaries public executed, issued passports , and the purpose of the Act of 1856, as we point out in our brief, was to restrict the issuance just to the Secretary of such rules and regulations as he might promulgate . QUESTION : But even after 1856, for many years, almost a century or more, a passport was not a travel control As we show historically in our brief, in the War of 1812, for was required for travel control, and this applied to persons The second time I believe it happened was at the time of the Civil War, when again a travel control statute was enacted . The third time was 1918, just after the First And part of our argument, of course, is that when each of these travel control statutes was passed, they assume that the President would be issuing and refusing to issue passports, because a travel control act would not make place, nor would it make sense if there was not the power to revoke or, indeed, to decline its issuance . And we contend that this is evidence that the Congress intended the Secretary of State to have the authorit which we insist he possessed here to revoke res pondent ' s passport .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, on the other hand , you can k i nd of turn that argument around and say that so long as a passport was no more than a letter of introduction, so to speak, that maybe it was in <:he discretion of the President not to give such a letter to anybody whose morals he didn ' t like , but it wouldn't affect that person very much. MR .· ?lcCREE: That's correct, and there have been instances when the President has endeavored to do that , just because he didn't aoprove of someone's polii:ical beliefs, for example .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. MR . McCREE: And that's the Kent case , where this Court without reaching the constitutional contention that was made there, held that a passport could not be denied a person because of his political affiliation. And later when the Congress attempted to do that, in Aptheker and Dayton this Court determined that it was unconstitutional to do it. We ' re not contending for thought control, or the a citizen ' s activities, not his thoughts, abroad are causing or are likely to cause serious damage to the national secu- rity and the foreign policy of the United States, that the having issued it, to revoke it . That ' s exactly what happened in this case when in in Teheran, and after this extraordinary statement at a press conference by the respondent, the Secretary of State notified respondent that his passport had been revoked and told him that he was entitled to a hearing under the appropriate regu- lat ion, and offered to hold an expedited hearing in West Germany where respondent lives .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And in this case the effect of the revocation of the passport was to bring the man home and make him stay there? To his home, back in our country? MR. McCREE : This would be the purpose of it . Or certainly to deprive him

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that wouldn't have been the effect of the revocation of a passport in the 19th century?

turns.name: HCCREE
turns.text: Without a travel control; that's cor- rect .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the 19th century?

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: Without a travel control statute, sidered in pari materia with this passport control . QUESTION : What, as a practical matter, happened when it was revoked? Did he turn it in and did he come back to the United States?· ·Does the record tell? MR . McCREE: I'm advised that he went to the con- sulate where he tendered it for validation, and it was taken up by the employee at the consulate, and he was given instead an identification card which would permit him to return home, but he cannot use for extended travels , and so physically his passport has been cancelled . QUESTION; But isn't the question whether he can use that card for exte nded travel a question of whatever law he , the country he seeks to travel to or from? MR . McCREE : It may be, because another country can of course, a sovereign country can have its own -- QUESTION : As far as American law is concerned , the only thing that -- it doesn't prejudice his ability to come back to the United States , and apparently he doesn't want to do that .

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: That's well, he may want to do that but in any event he could not

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if he did he couldn't get out again? MR . McCREE : He would not get out again. Also, it does not identify him as a person who is entitled to the -- anyone who's -- as of course the members of the Court have done, who have traveled internatio nally, recognize that it's better to be with a passport than without a passport . That's the issue but that ' s the only sanction which has been im- posed upon him for these activities here . QUESTION : Mr . Solicitor General , is there a speci- fie statute which permits revocatio n? MR . McCREE: There is no specific there is not . No, sir . There is not . In 1 926, July 3, the last of the series of statutes authorizing the President of the United States or the Secretary of State to issue passports , in ac- cordance .with rule• and · dded not contain either the power to refuse or t o revoke -- QUESTION : How long has there been a regulation which permits the Secretary to revoke? MR . McCREE: We -- in our -- QUESTION : There is n9w one , I take it?

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: Yes, the current one, I believe, was enacted in 1968, but there have been regulations and rules of the Department of State going back before the statute of 1856 . QUESTION : With respect to revocation? MR. McCREE : With respect to denial and revocation .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Covering both? our brief . For example , on page 25 of our brief, where we

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, has -- when was the Travel Act abroad, because they're getting into trouble. So we don't want anybody leaving the country and therefore we forbid the issuance of passports . Do you think

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: That would raise a consti tutional question, at a minimum, because this Court has h eld that the right to international travel is a constitutionally protected right, although entitled to lesser protection than intrastate travel . QUESTION : It ' s also held in Curtiss- Wright that the President is the sole organ of execution in the area of foreign affa irs, is it not? MR . McCREE : Well, that , of course, is the separa- tion of powers argument, which would also be involved were there to be such a situation as the Court has propounded . QUEST I ON : Well , I gather, Mr . Solicitor General , that as to the subject of revocation, insofar as there are any constitutiona l implications, it ' s irrelevant that Congres has ·n ot expressly given the Secretary of State power to revoke? MR . McCREE: Well, I would contend that . But we are not claiming for the purposes of this litigation the inherent power to revoke, because we QUESTION : Well, you ' re not suggesting either, are authority? MR . McCREE : Not at all , but we're willing to address this question as the Court of Appeals address ed it, which was to see whether the Congress impliedly granted the authority to revoke when it granted the authority t o grant. QUESTION : And so far , at least , knowing that there were such regulations, it never did anything to - -

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: It never took any steps .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In any way modify or repeal them or anything else? MR . McCREE : It never did with reference to either of these matters. QUESTION : And it most recently amended the statute in 1978 , didn ' t it? MR . McCREE : In 1978 it amended the Travel Control Act . QUESTION : And also limited the Secretary ' s power - MR . McCREE : And limited the area QUESTION : On ared restrictions . MR . McCREE: And it did not do this, being fully aware of these matters . QUESTION : So that the statutory authority is requisite; if it is at all, it's there . MR. McCREE : This is certainly our contention . were "participants in political affairs ... " Is there any record as to what the particular participation was in those instances?

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: In some instances there are, period when what is perhaps this century's "communist" and last century 's syndicalist or anarchist , where passports were denied for reasons of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Kent v . Dulles rather

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: But Kent that's right . QUESTION : Sort of required at that --

turns.name: MCCREE
turns.text: Kent v . Dulles , and Aptheker , and Dayton, threw all of that out. QUESTION : Well, does this case involve something more, like Kent v . Dulles, or are the areas of restriction similar? MR . McCREE: No , this case involves identifying a covert employee , an undercover employee of our intelligence service, and leaving ' him to the tender mercies of whatever might happen to a person so identified i n a country where we haven ' t the power to protect him . QUESTI?N : But he did nothing other than words . MR. McCREE : That's correct . He did nothing other than words, but these words were as deadly as bullets . book, identified 15 agents, the homes of two of them were attacked violently by armed men, and if words will precipi- tate that and they ' re uttered with the knowledge that they QUESTION : Well, could he be charged with a crime if he were in the United States and said that? MR . McCREE : He might. I suppose a holdup man who says, stick 'em up, and maybe doesn ' t brandish a gun at all -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If he doesn't have a gun, I don't know MR . McCREE: Well, I don't know , if he puts a per- son in fear of his life and extracts his wallet from him -- well -- I'm willing to say , if he talks over here the exact same words he's charged with talking over there , you couldn't do anything to him . MR . McCREE: You might not. But we are not claim- ing that he committed a crime that's indictable, that's an indictable offense . But we're claiming that these are activi ties that go beyond pure speech. This is not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that are not punishable here? MR . McCREE: It may not be punishable, we're not contending that it's punishable here, but we're saying that QUESTION : Then you have to say that taking his passport is not punishment . Don ' t you have to say that? MR . McCREE: It's a form of punishment, but it's not criminal punishment . It's an implementation of the national security. If this nation cannot have operatives abroad who can keep it advised of intelligence matters that may affect our security, certainly the safety of every one of us is diminished . QUESTION : General McCree, supposing a person right now were "to apply for a passport t o go to Salvador , and when asked the purpose of his journey , to say, to denounce the United States policy in Salvador in supporting the junta . And the Secretary of State says, I just will not issue a sistently do that in the light of our previous cases? MR . McCREE: I would say, yes, he can . Because we have to vest these -- The President of the United States and that we can exercise in this context . not, preaching communist doctrine?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It involved membership in the Communist Party , didn ' t it? MR . McCREE : Well , it was both, as I understand it . It was membership and policy

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's a preaching aspect of it , and there, I was thinking about my brother Rehnquist's hypotheti- cal to you . MR . McCREE: Oh, I think QUESTION : And their preaching communist doctrine he said you couldn ' t refuse a passport to go and do that . MR . McCREE : Because there was no showing , there was no contention -- QUESTION : And the differe nce from going to Sal - · vador? MR . McCREE: There was no contention made that that damaged t he national security . Now, if our national security

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How is the Salvador one involved in this MR . McCREE: Well, it just might, because it might be a provocation that would involve us militarily . For exam- ple -- and I ' m out of the record in answering this but jus Apparently they were some kinds of persons working under the cover of a labor organization, and if this person as undercover operatives and i t resulted i n t heir deaths, it seems to me a rule that would deprive the President of the United States from QUESTION : Well, that ' s something a little differ- ent than Justice Rehnquist's . But even if you couldn't re- trieve a pa ssport for the reason Justice Re hnq uis t s ugges ts, that doesn't cover this case where the threat is to the lives of other American citize ns . MR . McCREE : That ' s correct, Mr . Justice White . I f I may , I would like to reserve the balance of my time, if MR . CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER : Very well , Mr . Solicitor Ge neral . Mr . Wulf . MR . WULF: Mr . Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'm sorry Justice Rehnquist had to l eave because I did just want to start by specifically answering one fac - t ual question .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He ' ll be back before you finish your statement . MR . WULF: I just want to answer question whi ch asked the General that has to do wi th when in 1978, which was after that press release, and after his principal book was published in 1975 . This case involves the constitutional right t o travel. QUESTION : You say renewed . Do you also have the data on when it was issued?

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: I assume it was issued five years pre- viously thereto; I think five years is now the standard period .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your affidavit in the record says it was issued March 30, ' 73, and expires on March 29, 1983 . MR . WULF: That is an error . It was issued in '73. It expired in '7 8 -=-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it 's been renewed now until '8 3? MR . WULF: And is renewed

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm just reading from your affi- davit, that ' s all.

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: It must be a typographical error . I know that his passport expired, the one that was revoked, expired, was to expire in 1983. It was issued

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That ' s what you say. MR . WULF: Yes . Oh; I'm sorry . Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you say it was issued on March 30, '73? And do not suggest that it was renewed, but it was? MR . WULF: It was renewed in '78; yes. Because they ' re only good for five years . This case does involve the con stitutional right to Zemel , the power to issue passports is a power that ulti - mately is within the authority of the Congress of the Unite d ' States, and Congress mu st authorize either explicitly or implicitly any curtailment on the right to travel . The Government, of course, concedes that there's no explicit authorization in this case, and the task of this Court is to see whether it can divine from the history pre- sented to it by the Government and by ourselves as well whe - ther tnere is any implicit authorization which has to be based as the Court held in Kent and Zemel on a substantial and consistent administrative practice. I would like to just summarize our argument in a nutshell and then treat each of the separate arguments indi- vidually . Our first argument is that Kent itself disposes of-this case, because Kent, taken together with its companion case Dayton, in fact was a case which dealt with matters of national security and foreign policy considerations . It was not merely a case confined to speech and association, as the Government would have the Court see it. Two, should the Court disagree with our analysis there is a total absence of any substantial and consistent practice , which is required to find that Congress implicitly authorized the regulation involved in this case . Both of those arguments are supported by the fact that after Kent was decided by this Court in 1958, the then Administration tried very , very hard , and very , very unsuc- cessfuly to secure legislation which would authorize the very kind of authority which it has exercised in this case against Mr . Agee . And finally, looking around for what the intention of Congress actually is, we come to the amendments in 1978 , substantial amendments in 1978, of Section 2ll(a) , which is the fundamental passport authority, and Section 1185, which is the lineal descendent of all of the wartime national emergency provisions . QUESTION : Mr . Wulf, to what extent do you think that Congress can limit the President's authority to revoke or grant a passport to someone whom he has previously issued it to? MR. WULF : According to the decision in Kent, he has plenary -- Congress has plenary authority. The authority resides in Congress, it does not reside in the Executive Branch .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that if the President Italy and the President summons the ambassador home, that the ambassador can simply stay in Italy and keep his passport, saying I've got a right to travel and I ' m not going to come home? MR . WULF: Well , he can certainly be deposed and -- QUESTION : He wouldn't be ambassador for long , I ' m MR . WULF : Not at all . Yes . I think he can refuse to come home . In that case he would presumably have a diplomatic passport; he might have to turn that one in . He ' d be entitled to reissuance of a standar d passport if he was no longer ambassador . In 1978 , explicitly, Congress expressed what its intentions were, and its intenti ons there were to withdraw whatever authority the Court had allowed it to exercise in the area of geographical restrictions, in the Zemel case; and secondly, it revoked Section 1185 of the Title VIII, which was the travel control statute, and reversed the purpose of that statute 180 degrees and required , although it still re- quires that passports be used for entry and exit by citizens of the United States , the purpose is not to allow the Executive Branch to exercise travel control over citizens who hold passports , but to facilitate -- and that is e xplicitly what is in all of the congressional his t ory relating to 1185 now general requirement abroad that travelers present pass- ports upon entry to foreign countries .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which of our prior cases, Mr . Wulf, that of the respondent here? MR . WULF : I think that Kent involved the same kind of considerations that are involved here . That is to say --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Disclosure of national security infer- mation? MR . WULF: Well , the Kent decis i on , particularly the Kent dissent; and of course we.all remember the history of that period when travel by American communists and commun- ist sympathizers was prohibited, during the ' 50s. All of the expressions, all of the explanations about why that was being done had to do with considerations which were identical to the kind of national security and foreign policy concerns expressed by the Government in this case .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did any of the people involved in any of that litigation get themselves involved in releasing classi- fied information of the United States? which was a companion case to Kent, the basis on which his passport was refused at that time, which led to the decision here, was that it was thought that he had something to do -- wi th the Rosenberg espionage ring , and t hat he was at a meeting somewhere in New York where i n formation was prepared for delivery to a foreign nation . There was no charge -- or not founded , but how does that compare with the statements r eleased by Mr . Agee in t he London p r ess c onference? MR . WULF: Oh, I would think t h e y would be worse, Your Honor . QUESTION : Worse? MR . WULF : The statement s t h a t Mr . Agee released a re e x pression s , political expressions which are protected under t he First Amendment . There ' s no First Ame ndme nt protec t i on to engage in espionage or sabotage. I t hin k the Dayton case is bad , but I think that in the Government ' s terms Day ton is worse . Our brief essentially here today is that given the Government's best case, in its brief , in terms of the histori cal information which it has provided the Court, that this Court cannot confidently conclude that the Congress has im- plicitly authorized the power which the Government seeks to exercise here , that the Government's best case leaves it very doubtful, leaves it very ambiguous , leaves it very uncertain, about whether the kind of implicit authorization which is that this Court ought not guess about whether there is any such implicit --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, might I ask, Mr . Wulf, the same tion in Footnote 7 of the Court of Appeals' opinion? Is that the fact , do you know? It ' s at page l4a of the Petition for ten passport applications in '56 were refused because the applicants were "participants in political affairs abroad whose activities were deemed harmful to good relations . " Do you know what those cases are? MR . WULF : No , I don ' t know what they are , and neither does the Government, Your Honor, because there ' s absolutely no explanation about those in the documents where they appear.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, wouldn't that be significant in terms of the issue you're now arguing if that were so? MR . WULF: I think they are insignificant, Your Honor, because there's no explanation about what they mean . And they're as insignificant as are the other statistics which the Government has provided to the Court, because of th admixture of the kinds of -- I'm looking for the -- because of the different kinds of categories which those various passports were revoked, or ·refused . And it's in our of passports in six cases . At the same time there were per- sons whose previous conduct abroad has been such as to bring discredit on the United States and cause difficulty for other Americans, gave bad checks, left unpaid debts, had diffi- culties with police, et cetera . There's another category in '56 which refers to we ' re not told how many of those 36- odd were bad check, unpaid debt, difficulty with the police , and that sort of thing . The reason I asked the question was to see if we could find out . MR . WULF: We can't f i nd out , because they -- QUESTION : But that same statement says, there were numerous other refusals for security reasons, in addition to the ones that might be harmful to relations . MR . WULF: Well, their saying it isn't proof because -- well, they haven't produced it; perhaps they can but they haven't here, and they have to rely on the record which they presented to the Court here . The fact is that I think that those statistics are completely useless because of the totally inadequate description of what they were all about. I mean , if there are 56 such cases., 55 of them could have been for bad checks and one of them might have been for But I think that this Court can't make a decision based upon that kind of data which is just totally insufficient .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Am I disqualified to talk -- QUESTION : -- because I know why one of them was , am I disqualified because I know why one of them was?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Wulf, can I ask what your position is with respect to the authority of the Secretary of State to revoke a passport for matters such as writing bad checks, political in context, but is there authority for the Secretar to do it? States .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you agree that that's a proper basis for revocation of a passport, and if so, what's the authority for that? MR. WUL F: The authority for that is that Kent found that that was

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did Kent create an authority that did not previously exist?

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: Kent found the authority when it hadn't been previously looked for.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where did it find it? MR . WULF: In history.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In history, rather than in any written regulation or statute? MR . WULF: Absolutely; absolutely. Because the only statute is 2ll(a) which broadly confers the power to issue passports QUESTION : I'd sort of like to figure out where the thing started . I mean, where did the original power to revoke for that reason come from? There must have been a first case, when there would have been no prior history to justify it . MR . WULF: There were first cases in the 19th century which are described in one or another of the briefs QUESTION : Well , did Ke nt invol ve a revocation? MR . WULF : Kent was a refusal . Frankly , I lump them together, Your Honor .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know , so you do say that despite the absence of any e xpress power to revoke in t he Secretary, the Secretary at least for some reason may revoke a passport? MR . WULF: I wouldn ' t deny that . haven ' t argued that there ' s -- we haven't argued a distinction between a refusal to issue and a revocation . QUESTION : And if he can refuse for a reason he can revoke for the same reason? MR . WULF : We wouldn ' t cont est tha t; no . QUESTION : Well, are you suggest i ng now , Mr . vlulf, as in Kent, that Mr . Agee ' s activit i es have First Amendment protection? MR . WULF: Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then you reject MR . WULF: But I go beyond that, of course, because I think Kent is broader than merely a First Amendment case . We believe that Kent and Dayton, taken together, involve the same kind of national security- foreign relations concerns which are QUESTION : I but what was the constitutional protection for whatever the activities were that were involve MR . WULF: Yes . QUESTION : You reject the Court of Appeals ' dis - tinction, then? That there may be authority not to issue, there is no authority to revoke? MR . WULF : We don't argue the distinction about that, Your Honor .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr . Wulf, is it your position that the First Amendment protects everything that Mr . Agee is said to have stated in his press release in Londo n i n 1974 , including the e xposing of CIA agents abroad? MR . WULF : Yes , sir. It is our position that that is QUESTION : . there ' s no writ t en constitution i n England . MR . WULF : Oh, it ' s protected with respect to American law .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course . MR . WULF: Yes . Solicitor General has argued, resulted in their death, is tha a proper exercise of the First Amendment? MR . WULF: There is no claim at all here that his revelations have resulted in anybody's death . In fact, there ' s a specific disclaimer in one of the affidavits that's ll6a of the Petition for Cert .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not suggesting that there is proof of an agent dying as a result of Mr. Agee's disclosures; I just don't know . But I would think the occupation is suffi- ciently hazardous without having one's name publicly revealed

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: Well, as you presumably know, Your Honor there has been attempts continually being made for the last year or two in Congress to adopt a statute which.would make it a crime to identify CIA and otl'ier covert action --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It ' s very difficult to draw that sort of legislation.

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: And the Congress has been unsuccessful in doing it, and no bill has been acted on, no statute has been adopted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if it adopted -- HR . WULF: Until then, if such a statute should be adopted, rdare say that the question of constitutionality wil be here soon enough. In the meantime, I believe that that kind of identification is indeed protected by the First

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Wulf, suppose that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if the statute is otherwise valid? ever they may be? That ' s your position? MR. WULF : In general that is my position . In particular, I would say that Mr. Agee, like Mr . Snepp, to the same terms of the secrecy agreement that he executed. And in fact, he is now subject to that agreement. There is an injunction outstanding against him, which he is complying with. So whatever -- as of the past few months . So what- ever may have been the case before then, he is now bound by that injunction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Wulf , suppose then that Mr. Agee or somebody else applies for a passport and he says , I know I 'm subject to a secrecy agreement but I want to go to England and reveal the names of people that I am forbidden to reveal here, and if I go to England I can do it and probably get away with it. But I fully intend to breach my contract by passport. The remedy is not to deprive him of a passport. The remedy injunction. conference, could he be prosecuted over here? MR . WULF: No .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a violation of the law of Great Britain .

WULF: Well, the answer is that if he has so which is only a crime by the law of a foreign state, he QUESTION : Well, suppose it's not a crime by the law of the foreign state? I think that ' s Justice Stewart's MR . WULF: If it's a crime by the law of the United States, he can be prosecuted here, of course .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though the conduct's committed abroad, where it ' s not a crime? MR . WULF: It might in some circumstances . I think there are some extraterritorial probl ems the law you referred to and made it a cri me to do that, and a person applied for a passport and the Secretary says, what are you -- somehow the Secretary knew and he freely could he refuse a passport? MR . WULF : I said I do not believe so, and I believ his remedy is in the criminal law, or if there ' s an injunc - tion , for contempt . QUESTION : Get an injunction before he gets on the p l ane . MR . WULF: Perhaps they can get an i njunction to en force the injunction against him prohi bi t i ng him from re- vealing information . But apart from that, I believe that there is no authority to withhold his passport .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you think that the in personam restraint of an American court injunction would follow hi m in London or Beirut or wherever? An in personam -- MR . WULF: It might well, it might well. I c ould see that kind of an injunction which is breached abroad being basis for a contempt citation here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr . Wulf, putting to one side -- MR . WULF: That wouldn't shock me very much . I mus say, though, that this concentration on the facts of this particular case, I beg your leave , doesn't have anything to I can understand why the Government has concentrated on the facts in this case in trying to make QUESTION : Mr . Wulf , let 's put these facts to one preexisting contracts restraints or violations of law ; just the conduct of an American citizen while abroad . Does the United States have any control over what a citizen may do other than by trying to restrict his right to travel by re- voking his passport? If it has any . Maybe it has none, which is essentiaily your position --

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: I think it has none unless there is an extraterritorial crime has been committed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if it has, other than extraterri- torial crime , which is a rather there are instances, of course, but other than that, if it is to impose any meaning- ful control over what our citizens abroad may do that may impact on national security or anything else, it must be through this device, must it not? MR . WULF : Yes , it must be . But the issue today is whether Congress declares that it will be . I mean, that's th issue today .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well , I know , but precisely, the issue is whether the regulation issued pursuant to the statute that Congress did enact was within the power of the President as MR · WULF·: No, I disagree . The issue is, whether there was any authority for the regulation at all .

WULF: I might take a different position, but it doesn ' t say that, of course , so I don't have to take any position . QUESTION : I take it all of your answers exclude " in time of declared war" ? MR . WULF : We don ' t deny that in times of declared war there can be restrictions, limitations imposed on travel which would not be unconstitutional, and of course Congress has adopted such legislation in 1918, 1941, and related legislation in 1952 . But none of that is in effect now, of course, including the 1952 legislation,since that natio nal emergency is no longer in effect . There is no national emergency. Our second argument, of course, goes to the ques- tion whether there is any proof presented, historical proof presented to you by the Government which would persuade you that there is the necessary consistent history of zation . QUESTION : How many times in the past have there American passport ho l der? You were talking about a history , tory . Are there others? MR . WULF : Well, the Government describes seven in its brief , extending from 1906 to 1970, and then, of cours , it also lumps together those other QUESTION : Twenty- odd . MR . WULF : Well, there were 62 in their opening brief . It turns out to be 100 in their reply nondescript .cases outofthe statistics . Specifically they refer to seven cases in the last 75 years which they claim They ' re in the brief and it was only one of them, in 1970, which was in fact under the same regulation . The others weren' t under a ny regulation at all , as a matter of fact . It was -- t hey were just under the claimed discretion of the Secretary of State . As I said in opening, I think that these examples of administrative practice presented to the Court by the Government are far too ambiguous, far too uncertain a basis upon which this Court ought to rest its judgment that they

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Implicit authorization to do what? MR . WULF: Implicit authorization for the regula-

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: To refuse passports

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because you agree that there is a power to revoke?

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: Yes . Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For the right reasons?

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: Quite; quite right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And there's a historical practice of revoking

turns.name: WULF
turns.text: Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But ndt for the reasons claimed? MR . WULF: Precisely; yes . And with that and the Court ought not make that decision on the basis of the evidence here . That decision ought to be, the Court ought to require, in light of the fact that this is a consti- tutional right, that Congress exclusively say what its in- tentions are with respect to this kind of power which is asserted in the regulation before the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, anyway, Mr. Wulf, you would argue that if there were explicit statutory authorization to re- voke for this kind of conduct, the statute to that extent Amendment protection? MR . WULF: I would make that argument, but then we would deal squarely with the constitutionality in First Amendment terms of the authority which was concededly granted to the Executi ve Branch . But today we don' t believe that that authority exists . I think that the 1978 amendments There Congress revoked what authority this Court had given to the Government in the Zemel case regarding withdraw whatever authority the Executive Branch had with respect to nati onal security and foreign policy considera- tions. We believe that the decision below should be affirmed.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr . Solicitor General, do you have any -- ? MR . McCREE: Mr . Chief Justice, and may it please the Court : If I have 60 seconds left I'd like to call the Court's attention to two matters. First, in response to counsel's claim about the effect and the intent of the 1978 amendments, I would refer you to page 47 of our brief, where we set forth the Senate report which makes it clear, and "The Committee recognizes clearly that the passport authority should not be restricted in any way which would limit the President's ability to control the departure of United States citizens to fore i gn countries when such travel is inconsis- tent with a greater Government interest . " The other matter to which I would like to direct the Court's attention, if I may, is on this seven-page reply memorandum that we filed to the Petition for Certiorari, where we set forth the colloquy conducted by the court and counsel in the district court, where counsel under very careful questioning concedes that for the purpose of the determination of the validity of this regulation, he concedes that his client was causing or likely to cause serious damage to the national security . And I suggest, therefore, that he admits that he is fully within the ambit of this regulation which means that this Court needn't decide the question whether his conduct was within the regulation. Of course, it leaves the constitutional question as well as the statutory authorization question to be determined. And with that, we would submit our case on the briefs. MR . CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER: Thank you, gentlemen, the case is submitted.
